chain_of_thought: Crash shows only abort → __stack_chk_fail → unknown frames, with no resolved application-level native function below the stack protector; allocator/sanitizer frames precede any identifiable app code.

Library map indicates the relevant JNI library is libcortana-jni.so with JNI entry Java_com_microsoft_bing_cortana_jni_CortanaJni_decodeStreamingAudio, but the crash stack does not expose this symbol or any other concrete function name/address to analyze in Ghidra.

Because no concrete native frame name or address is available, step 1 of the mandatory analysis (identify first app-level frame below allocators/sanitizers) is impossible to satisfy in practice; consequently, steps 2–5 (decompilation, data-flow tracing, JNI/Java argument analysis) cannot be performed on specific code locations.

Java-side call graph shows a realistic production path (CommuteStreamingDecoder.decodeOnce → run → ConversationImpl.decodeStreamingAudio), so this is not obviously a pure fuzz-only path, but without native frames we cannot link the abort to any particular misuse of memcpy, pointer arithmetic, or other memory-unsafe operations.

__stack_chk_fail is triggered when a stack canary check fails; this usually implies some form of stack buffer overflow in native code, but can also arise from tooling, corrupted stacks due to harness misconfiguration, or prior memory corruption elsewhere. Without a visible native function, we cannot attribute this to attacker-controlled input versus internal logic/harness issues.

ConversationImpl.decodeStreamingAudio takes a byte[] and a ByteBuffer, which could plausibly be influenced by untrusted data (e.g., audio stream); however, in the absence of decompiled JNI and native frames we cannot show that lengths or pointers derived from these arguments are misused or insufficiently validated.

Given the complete lack of app-level stack details and inability to inspect the native implementation, classifying this as a concrete, code-level vulnerability would be speculative; the safest stance is that the presence of __stack_chk_fail suggests potential memory corruption but is not proven exploitable or even reproducibly reachable from legitimate app use.
reasons: Stack trace stops at __stack_chk_fail with no identifiable application-level native function or address, preventing concrete code analysis.
Abort via stack canary failure suggests memory corruption but could stem from fuzz/harness artifacts or non-reproducible corruption without evidence of attacker-controlled influence.
No native frames, memcpy-style calls, or pointer/length handling can be inspected, so a concrete vulnerability cannot be established.
recommendations: Re-run the fuzzing with symbols or addr2line support to capture concrete native function names/addresses below __stack_chk_fail.
Instrument libcortana-jni.so with ASan/UBSan and re-fuzz decodeStreamingAudio to obtain a precise report of the overflowing operation.

Add logging or assertions in Java_com_microsoft_bing_cortana_jni_CortanaJni_decodeStreamingAudio around stack allocations and copies derived from the input byte[] and ByteBuffer to narrow down the fault.
assumptions: No additional symbolized native stack trace is available beyond what is shown.
The fuzz harness may be able to pass arguments that differ from realistic production use (e.g., extreme sizes, nulls).
limitations: Unable to perform required Ghidra MCP decompilation because no app-level native frame/address is present in the stack trace.
JNI implementation of decodeStreamingAudio in libcortana-jni.so is not visible, so argument handling and bounds checks cannot be inspected.
No sanitizer (ASan/UBSan) output available to correlate the stack canary failure with a specific operation or buffer.
